rationale: The citing paper uses Kivetz, Netzer, and Srinivasan (2004) as part of the core context-effects foundation, specifically when framing compromise effects as a robust phenomenon. It also explicitly references that paper's call for more work on heterogeneity in context effects and then conducts exploratory heterogeneity analyses in that spirit. Although the citing study does not adopt the cited paper's specific alternative compromise-effect model structures, the citation plays a meaningful conceptual and positioning role beyond a passing mention.
relation_description: The 2010 study builds its context-effects framing partly on Kivetz et al. (2004), using it to justify the relevance of compromise effects and to motivate additional heterogeneity analysis. The cited work functions as conceptual grounding rather than as a directly reused model implementation.
